coredata.dc:title: Nitrogen-doped carbon dots as a fluorescent probe for folic acid detection and live cell imaging
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Liu, Baoqiang
Wei, Shanshan
Liu, Enqi
Zhang, Hongyuan
Lu, Pengju
Wang, Jiali
Sun, Guoying
coredata.dc:description: The folic acid (FA) level in human body can be used as an indicator for body's normal physiological activities and offer insight into the growth and reproduction of the body's cells. But the abnormal level of FA can cause some diseases. Herein, we designed a simple and convenient approach to prepare fluorescent N-doped carbon dots (N-CDs) for the FA detection. These N-CDs have excellent hydrophilicity, high photostability, and outstanding biocompatibility, as well as excitation-independent emission behavior with typical excitation/emission peaks at 295 nm/412 nm. Upon the existence of FA, the fluorescence emission spectrum of N-CDs was significantly quenched through the synergy of static quenching mechanism and internal filtering effect (IFE). Under optimal conditions, the limit of detection was 28.0 nM (S/N = 3) within the FA concentration range of 0–200.0 μM. In addition, N-CDs were successfully employed to detect FA in real samples such as urine and fetal bovine serum (FBS), with a recovery rate of 99.6%-100.7% for quantitative addition. Furthermore, cell experiments confirmed the low toxicity and the cell imaging performance of these N-CDs, indicating that the obtained N-CDs could be served as a credible quantitative probe for FA analysis in the field of biosensing.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Folic acid
Fluorescence probe
Static quenching mechanism
Internal filtering effect
Cell imaging

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Application of N-CDs in FA determination in actual samples

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Jilin Provincial Science and Technology Department

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Jilin Province Science and Technology Development Project

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This study was supported by the Jilin Province Science and Technology Development Project (20170101094JC).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM image (inset: HR-TEM lattice fringes), (b) size distribution, (c) AFM image, and (d) FT-IR spectrum of N-CDs.
Full-survey XPS of N-CDs (a). High-resolution XPS of C 1 s (b), N 1 s (c), and O 1 s (d).

(a) Excitation, emission, and absorption spectra of N-CDs. Inset: pictures of N-CDs under sunlight (left) and UV lamp (right). (b) Emission spectra under various excitation wavelength (245 nm-345 nm). Effects of fluorescence intensity with different NaCl concentrations (c) and UV irradiation times (d).

(a) Response of N-CDs toward FA and interfering substances. (b) Impact of pH on the fluorescence of N-CDs. (c) Impact of different FA concentrations (0 μM, 10.0 μM, 20.0 μM, 30.0 μM, 40.0 μM, 50.0 μM, 60.0 μM, 70.0 μM, 80.0 μM, 90.0 μM, 100.0 μM, 150.0 μM, and 200.0 μM) on the spectra of N-CDs. (d) Linear between F0/F-1 and FA concentration.
(a) Lifetimes of N-CDs and FA/N-CDs. (b) Fluorescence spectra, (c) absorbance spectra, and (d) quenching mechanism of N-CDs.
Confocal microscopic images of cells cultured for 4 h (a-c), 12 h (d-f), and 24 h (g-i) with 100.0 μg mL−1 of N-CDs.
Preparation of N-CDs and the fluorescence “turn-off” detection of FA.
Different fluorescent sensors reported for FA detection.
Determination of FA in urine and FBS samples.

head.ce:title: Nitrogen-doped carbon dots as a fluorescent probe for folic acid detection and live cell imaging

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Life Science, Changchun University of Technology, 2055 Yanan Street, Changchun 130012, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Life Science

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemistry and Life Science, Changchun University of Technology, 2055 Yanan Street, Changchun 130012, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Advanced Institute of Materials Science, Changchun University of Technology, 2055 Yanan Street, Changchun 130012, PR China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Advanced Institute of Materials Science, Changchun University of Technology, 2055 Yanan Street, Changchun 130012, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A sensitive blue fluorescent probe based on carbon dots has been synthesized by a one-pot hydrothermal method for the rapid detection of FA.

The folic acid (FA) level in human body can be used as an indicator for body's normal physiological activities and offer insight into the growth and reproduction of the body's cells. But the abnormal level of FA can cause some diseases. Herein, we designed a simple and convenient approach to prepare fluorescent N-doped carbon dots (N-CDs) for the FA detection. These N-CDs have excellent hydrophilicity, high photostability, and outstanding biocompatibility, as well as excitation-independent emission behavior with typical excitation/emission peaks at 295 nm/412 nm. Upon the existence of FA, the fluorescence emission spectrum of N-CDs was significantly quenched through the synergy of static quenching mechanism and internal filtering effect (IFE). Under optimal conditions, the limit of detection was 28.0 nM (S/N = 3) within the FA concentration range of 0–200.0 μM. In addition, N-CDs were successfully employed to detect FA in real samples such as urine and fetal bovine serum (FBS), with a recovery rate of 99.6%-100.7% for quantitative addition. Furthermore, cell experiments confirmed the low toxicity and the cell imaging performance of these N-CDs, indicating that the obtained N-CDs could be served as a credible quantitative probe for FA analysis in the field of biosensing.

ce:section.ce:para: Folic acid (FA) is an essential vitamin for human body. As a cofactor of enzymes, it plays a pivotal role in various physiological metabolic processes in all organisms such as the synthesis of proteins and nucleic acids [1]. It is also a key component of the hematopoietic system and is closely connected to anemia with leukopenia [2]. A lot of researches suggest that FA can disturb the cellular homeostasis and induce a variety of biological dysfunction, including cardiovascular disease, neural tube defects, senile dementia, autism, and Alzheimer's disease [3]. Additionally, the deficiency of FA in the brain can result in death from stroke and the insufficient intake of FA during pregnancy can cause fetalanomaly [4]. The World Health Organization (WHO) has formulated regulation on the intake of FA (0.4 mg/day) in daily life [5]. Owing to the important impact on human body, it is urgently desired to design a sensitive and convenient strategy to detect FA [1,6].

Nowadays, a variety of FA quantitative analysis strategies have been developed, such as enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay (ELISA), high performance liquid chromatography (HPLC), electrochemical analysis (EC), and microbiological assay (MA). For example, Faraji and colleagues achieved a study of HPLC using vortex-assisted dispersive liquid–liquid microextraction method (VA-DES-DLME), which was suitable for the quantitative analysis of FA [7]. In 2016, Kong et al. developed an ELISA test strip on the basis of monoclonal antibody for FA detection in beverages and milk samples [8]. In addition, Zengin and co-workers also utilized molecularly imprinted PDMS elastomer to detect FA in orange juice [9]. Although these methods can provide reliable detection results, most of these approaches require sophisticated equipment, complex processing steps, and a lot of time. Therefore, the more convenient and faster strategy to analyze and detect the level of FA still needs to be further explored.

In recent years, fluorescence analysis has attracted much attention due to its rapidness, simplicity, excellent selectivity, and high sensitivity. And a lot of fluorescent sensing platforms for FA determination were designed. For example, a high luminescence nanomaterial was designed for quantitative analysis of FA by using GSH functionalized Mn2+-doped CdTe/ZnS quantum dots (QDs) in 2021. In the project, the LOD of FA was calculated to be 0.2 μM [10]. Li et al. developed the ovalbumin stabilized copper nanoclusters (OVA-CuNCs) by using the commonly used protein stabilization strategy to realize the sensitive detection of FA [11]. In 2020, Yang and collaborators also applied a water-stable MOF-AgClO4-abtz as fluorescent platform for the rapid and efficient FA determination in serum and plasma samples [12]. However, most of probe materials have the disadvantages of complicated preparation process, low sensitivity, narrow detection range, and high toxicity. As a new class of high-efficiency fluorescent nanoprobe, carbon dots (CDs) exhibit more excellent water solubility, resistance to photobleaching, easy modification and low toxicity compared with traditional QDs and fluorescent dyes. These advantages provide a broad promise for applications of CDs in biological systems. As a result, CDs have presented many potential applications in biosensing and bioimaging, and a lot of CD-based probes for various analytes have been applied. However, only a few CD-based fluorescent probes for FA detection have been reported. Mu et al. used the N, S, I-doped CDs to determine FA. The work provided a LOD (84.0 nM) for FA through the fluorescence resonance energy transfer [13]. In 2020, the optical CDs synthesized using Hyaluronic acid was used to rapidly detect of FA (4.0 μM-100.0 μM) with a LOD of 0.49 μM [14]. Many of the previously reported methods generally involved complex preparation conditions, difficult modification procedures, and low detection limits. Therefore, it is still imperative to establish a high-sensitivity fluorescent probe for FA detection.

Herein, we first used salicylic acid as raw material to obtain N-CDs through a one-step hydrothermal route for the selective detection of FA (Scheme 1 ). This method does not require expensive or highly toxic reagents, strong acids, and further functionalization. The obtained N-CDs displayed strong blue fluorescence, good light stability, negligible cytotoxicity, and excellent biocompatibility. These N-CDs also displayed high sensitivity for the detection of FA and had lower LOD (28 nM) compared with other works. Moreover, the result of the FA recovery test in real samples demonstrated that the FA determination could be achieved in urine and FBS samples. The synergistic mechanism of static quenching and IFE of FA detection was demonstrated by examining spectral characteristics and fluorescence lifetime of N-CDs. Finally, the FA determination in living cells has been achieved by measuring the blue fluorescence intensity of N-CDs. Thus, the probe reported in this work exhibited considerable potential for biodetection due to their wonderful biocompatibility and stable optical characteristics.

NaCl, LiCl, CaCl2, CuCl2, MgCl2, and FeSO4 were provided by Aladdin (China). Arginine (Arg), histidine (His), glutathione (GSH), glucose (Glu), and L-cysteine (L-Cys) were bought from the Macklin (China). Salicylic acid, urea, and 3-(4,5)-dimethylthiahiazo(-2)-3,5-diphenytetrazoliumromide (MTT) assay kit were obtained from Sigma-Aldrich (USA). 4 T1 cells (mouse breast cancer cells) were attained from the Chinese National Infrastructure of Cell Line Resource. The ultrapure water used in this work was purified through Millipore ultrapure water instrument.

The morphology and crystallization property of N-CDs were investigated on a high-resolution transmission electron microscope (HR-TEM, Talos F200C). A multi-mode atomic force microscope (AFM, Dimension ICON2-SYS) was used to collect the thickness distribution of N-CDs. The size distribution of N-CDs was performed by dynamic light scattering (DLS, Malvern). The X-ray diffraction (XRD) spectrum of N-CDs was recorded on a diffractometer (Smart Lab-9 kW) with the scanning range from 10° to 80°. Under dark condition, a Raman spectrometer (LABRAM HR Evolution) was used to analyze the constitution of N-CDs. The examination of functional groups on N-CDs surface was achieved by Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR, Is50). The element content and valence of N-CDs were analyzed by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, X'Pert3 MRD). Absorption and fluorescence spectrum were scanned with Cary50 instrument and PerkinElmer-LS-55, respectively. The FDU-2110 instrument was used for freeze-drying N-CDs. The cell images were observed on confocal microscope (Leica DMI4000B).

The N-CDs were synthesized via a facile hydrothermal route. In a typical preparation process, 0.4 g salicylic acid and 0.5 g urea were added to a Teflon-lined autoclave containing 15.0 mL Ultra-pure water and heated for 1 h at 200 °C. After the resulting solution was cooled to room temperature, it was transferred into a dialysis bag (MWCO: 300 Da), and dialyzed for 5 h in a continuously stirred aqueous environment to remove impurities in the solution. Then the light yellow N-CDs solution was obtained via a 0.22-μm filter to purify, and freeze-dried for later utilization.

The QuantaMaster 8000 fluorescence spectrometer was used to measure the QY of N-CDs. First, the aqueous solution was added into a fluorescence cuvette as the reference solution, and the fluorescence spectrum was collected. Then the fluorescence spectrum of the N-CDs solution was collected under the same conditions. Finally, the QY of N-CDs was calculated on basis of the integral area between the fluorescence spectra of the aqueous solution and the N-CDs solution.

In a typical assay, 100.0 μL solution (pH = 7.4) containing various concentrations of FA was added to 300.0 μL N-CDs solutions and then shaken for 5 min. With the sample volume unchanged (3.0 mL), the FA concentrations in the mixed samples were 0–200.0 μM. Finally, under the excitation at 295 nm, the spectra of all samples were acquired with a spectrofluorimeter instrument. In addition, the selectivity for FA detection was tested in a similar way, just instead of FA with other interfering substances (Li+, Na+, Ag+, Mg2+, Ca2+, Cu2+, Fe2+, Mn2+, Zn2+, Pb2+, Co2+, Hg2+, Fe3+, Cr3+, Cl-, NO3 –, SO4 2-, AC-, Arg, His, L-Cys, L-GSH, UA, and Glu).

To explore the practicability of N-CDs in real samples, a spike-recovery analysis of FA in urine and FBS was investigated. Usually, urine samples were derived from healthy humans. Urine and FBS samples were treated by centrifugation (10,000 rpm for 5 min) and filtration (0.22 μm microporous membrane). Then, urine and FBS samples were diluted 100-fold and mingled with the N-CDs under the identical situation. After an appropriate volume of the sample solution spiked with FA at three concentrations of 20.0 μM, 30.0 μM, and 40.0 μM was mixed into the N-CDs solution, the emission spectra of the mixed system were detected. Ultimately, the recoveries and relative standard deviations (RSDs) of urine and FBS spiked samples were counted.

The standard MTT assay was applied to ascertain cytotoxicity in 4 T1 cells. Briefly, cells were incubated with DMEM containing 10.0% FBS, 1.0% anlimycoplasnia antibiotics and in an incubator (5.0% CO2) for 24 h at 37 ℃. The adherent cells were treated by DMEM containing different concentrations (0–500.0 µg mL−1) of N-CDs and incubated for 24 h. Next, the cells of each well were handled with 100.0 µL MTT (5.0 mg mL−1) and cultivated for 4 h. After the supernatant was removed, 100.0 µL DMSO was mixed into plate to dissolve the crystals. Finally, the optical density (OD) was quantified at 490 nm using the microplate reader, and the activity of cells was assessed as a percentage.
C e l l v i a b i l i t y
100 %
=
t h e a b s o r b a n c e o f s a m p l e s - t h e a b s o r b a n c e o f b l a n k
t h e a b s o r b a n c e o f c o n t r o l g r o u p - t h e a b s o r b a n c e o f b l a n k
× 100 %

The capability of our proposed N-CDs to monitor the FA level of living cells was evaluated by confocal imaging experiments. Initially, 4 T1 cells were inoculated in bottom of the cell culture flask and incubated for 24 h at 37 °C using DMEM containing 10.0% FBS and 1.0% anlimycoplasnia antibiotics. Subsequently, 4 T1 cells were washed with PBS 3 times and treated with 100.0 μg mL−1N-CDs solution. After overnight, images of these 4 T1 cells were observed under confocal fluorescence microscope (excitation/emission wavelength = 330 nm/410 nm).

The N-CDs were synthesized through the carbonization of salicylic acid and urea at 200 °C for 1 h using a facile hydrothermal method (Fig. S1). TEM, AFM, and DLS were applied to characterize the morphology and size distribution of them. As Fig. 1 a depicted, the image of TEM clearly presented that these N-CDs were spherical in shape and had good monodispersity with the average diameter of 2.17 ± 0.38 nm (Fig. 1b). The lattice fringes spacing of 0.21 nm was clearly displayed in HR-TEM image, which corresponded to the (100) graphitic in-plane lattice [15]. DLS spectrum illustrated that the diameter for N-CDs was within 1.1–4.5 nm (Fig. S2). Additionally, a typical AFM image represented that the height of N-CDs was 1.0–5.1 nm (Fig. 1c), corresponding well to the results of TEM and DLS [16].

As shown in a typical XRD pattern, a broad peak was observed at 2θ = 24.87°, which matched the (002) facet of the graphite carbon (Fig. S3) [17]. In the FT-IR spectrum (Fig. 1d), two characteristic peaks at 3400 cm−1 and 3190 cm-1could be attributed to the stretching vibration of the N-H and O-H bonds [18]. The peak at 2935 cm−1 was derived from the C-H anti-symmetric stretching vibration [19]. Four peaks at 1600 cm−1, 1405 cm−1, 1275 cm−1, and 1027 cm−1 corresponded to the stretching vibrations of C = O, C = N, C-N, and C-O, respectively [20]. A new peak derived from the N-H bond stretching vibration could be observed by comparing the FT-IR of salicylic acid and N-CDs (Fig. S4), indicating that the N elements had been successfully doped in these N-CDs. Raman spectrum was also utilized for the investigation of the structural characteristics of N-CDs. Under 532 nm excitation, two distinct signal peaks at 1377 cm−1 and 1618 cm−1 corresponded to the disordered D and the graphite G band (Fig. S5)[21]. Additionally, a new signal band at 1773 cm−1 appeared, which might belong to the carbonyl bending vibration of salicylic acid [22]. The above results confirmed that large amounts of amino, hydroxyl, and carbonyl groups were located on the surface of N-CDs [23]. Thus, these N-CDs were expected to have good water solubility owing to the presence of these hydrophilic groups, which could provide many advantages for the application in the biomedicine field.

Afterwards, XPS analyses were performed to explain the element composition and the surface functional groups of N-CDs. The predominant peaks at 285.1 eV, 399.6 eV, and 532.3 eV were observed in the XPS full spectrum of N-CDs, which corresponded to the binding energies of C 1 s, N 1 s, and O 1 s, respectively (Fig. 2 a). The result illustrated that these synthesized N-CDs were mainly consisted of C (60.34%), O (20.32%), and N (19.34%) atoms. The nitrogen/carbon ratio was counted to be 32.05%, manifesting a higher N-doping in these N-CDs. Furthermore, the C 1 s XPS spectrum of N-CDs was resolved into three characteristic peaks at 284.6 eV, 285.7 eV, and 288.4 eV, which were related to the binding energies of C = C/C-C, C-N, and C = O, respectively (Fig. 2b) [24]. Two distinct peaks at 399.5 eV and 400.6 eV were ascribed to N-H and C-N groups in the N 1 s spectrum, respectively (Fig. 2c) [25]. The O 1 s spectrum could be approximately divided into two different peaks at 531.4 eV and 532.3 eV, which might be attributed to C = O and C-O bonds, respectively (Fig. 2d) [26]. From the above analysis, it could be inferred that N had been successfully doped in N-CDs and plenty of C, N and O atoms existed in the N-CDs. Because the emission could be effected by high nitrogen-doped, these N-CDs were expected to have good luminescence properties and biomedical applications prospect.

Absorption spectrum and fluorescence spectrum were surveyed to research the optical characteristics of N-CDs. The N-CDs solution diluted with water was almost colorless under sunlight and displayed strong blue fluorescence under UV lamp (Fig. 3 a), showing that the N-CDs expressed an outstanding optical characteristic. Two peaks at 230 nm and 300 nm exhibited in the absorption spectrum, belonging to the π → π* electron transition of the C = C in an aromatic domain and the n → π* electron transition of the C = O, respectively (Fig. 3a) [27]. Under the 295 nm excitation, the maximum emission of N-CDs was detected at 412 nm (Fig. 3a). The position of the maximum emission did not shift when the excitation wavelength changed from 245 nm to 345 nm likely due to the surface molecular state emission mechanism (Fig. 3b)[28]. This constant emission further signified that the as-prepared N-CDs only contained a single emitter and had a very uniform particle size and surface distribution state [29]. In addition, the fluorescence QY was measured to be 26.5% using solvent water as reference.

Before N-CDs were used to sense FA in biological systems, the influence of UV radiation and NaCl on the optical property of N-CDs was first investigated. No precipitation appeared in the N-CDs solution even though the NaCl concentration reached 1.0 M (Fig. 3c), indicating that N-CDs had excellent stability in high ionic media. The fluorescence emission wavelength hardly changed after stored for 5 months at ordinary temperature. Moreover, the fluorescence intensity did not decrease under the same experimental conditions after continuous irradiation with a UV lamp at 365 nm for 5 h (Fig. 3d), demonstrating the excellent photostability of these N-CDs. The powder of lyophilized N-CDs could be redistributed in aqueous solution without any agglomeration. After stored in the refrigerator (4 ℃) for about one year, the N-CDs solution was still clear without any impurities.

Apart from the NaCl test, the pH-induced fluorescence behavior of N-CDs also had great significance on the perspective of application. To get more insight to the influence of pH on N-CDs, the fluorescence intensity of N-CDs in different pH aqueous solutions was examined. It gradually decreased with the reducing of pH from 6.0 to 1.0, while the fluorescence intensity remained stable from pH 7.0 to pH 14.0 (Fig. 4 b). The weaker fluorescence intensity in the extremely acidic region (pH = 1.0–4.0) and the drastic change of the fluorescence from pH 5.0 to pH 7.0 were observed, and this pH-dependent fluorescence behavior was reversible. This quenching phenomenon caused by pH might be due to the aggregation of N-CDs particles[30]. The changing surface charge in protonation and deprotonation could induce this pH response, which further demonstrated that these obtained N-CDs were co-functionalized with carboxyl and amine groups[31]. These results manifested the remarkable stability of N-CDs under physiological pH circumstances, which was conducive to the detection of intracellular molecular substances.

Because the complex intracellular system contained diverse interfering species, the selectivity test was accomplished before cellular application. Under the same conditions, various ions (Li+, Na+, Ag+, Mg2+, Ca2+, Cu2+, Fe2+, Mn2+, Zn2+, Pb2+, Co2+, Hg2+, Fe3+, Cr3+, Cl-, NO3 –, SO4 2-, and AC-) and some small biomolecules (Arg, His, L-Cys, L-GSH, UA, and Glu) commonly found in living creature were mixed into the N-CDs solution with and without FA. The FA detection experiments were all carried out at pH = 7.4, because FA was unstable in acidic solution and relatively stable in neutral or basic solution [32]. In addition, the pH of normal cells in human body is also 7.4, which is convenient for subsequent cell experiments. As Fig. 4a depicted, the blue fluorescence emission was quenched about 95.0% by FA (200.0 μM). Meanwhile, there was almost no significant decrease on the fluorescence of N-CDs even if the concentration of the above mentioned interference species (except Fe3+) reached 0.1 M. Fortunately, the signal of Fe3+ could be eliminated by triethanolamine [33]. Anti-interference experiments were also performed on the composite system of N-CDs and FA (Fig. S6). No obvious fluorescence change of the composite system was caused by interfering substances. Therefore, the excellent anti-interference ability indicated that N-CDs possessed significant selectivity for FA determination.

The feasibility of N-CDs for FA detection was then explored by fluorescence spectroscopy under the optimized conditions. As Fig. 4c illustrated, the maximum emission of the N-CDs solution gradually diminished after incubated with various concentrations of FA, indicating that FA could be sensitively detected by N-CDs from 0 to 200.0 μM. The Stern-Vollmer equation was used to simulate the quenching effect [34].
(2)
(
F 0
/ F )
- 1 =
K s
× c

where F0 and F mean the fluorescence intensities at 412 nm with and without FA. Ks is defined as the quenching constant of the equation. (F0/F)-1 and c represent the fluorescence quenching degree and FA concentration, respectively. (F0/F)-1 demonstrates linear relationship (R2 = 0.996) versus FA concentration from 0 μM to 200.0 μM (Fig. 4d). And the LOD was calculated to be 28.0 nM in line with the 3 s (signal-to-noise) criteria, which was lower than other reports for FA determination (Table 1 ). The reaction between N-CDs and FA could rapidly accomplish within 3 min (Fig. S7). Thus, these fast-response N-CDs could be served as a nanoprobe to track FA in biological systems.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application of N-CDs in FA determination in actual samples
ce:section.ce:para: To research the applicability and validity of these as-prepared N-CDs, actual samples with different FA concentration (20.0 μM, 30.0 μM, and 40.0 μM) were examined. The recovery and relative standard deviation (RSD) were measured by spiking the urine samples and FBS samples with FA in different concentrations in the range of the linearity. The obtained recovery of FA in the urine and FBS samples was between 99.6% and 100.7% with RSD in the range of 2.4%-2.9% (Table 2 ), which indicated the accuracy of these N-CDs to determine the FA concentration in actual samples. Therefore, these N-CDs could be utilized for the real-time FA determination as the high-efficiency fluorescent probe.

It is great significant for the strategic design of sensing systems to search the quenching mechanism behind the detection behavior of fluorescent probes. To deeper understand the mechanism of FA detection, the absorption and the average lifetime of N-CDs had been tested. The average lifetime before and after adding FA was approximately 4.43 ns and 4.19 ns, respectively (Fig. 5 a). This negligible change of two different analytes was unreasonably ascribed to the dynamic quenching [39]. Furthermore, under the conditions of various concentrations (0–100.0 μM) of FA, the decay curves of N-CDs still remained stable (Fig. S8), demonstrating that the quenching mechanism might be static quenching. Absorption of N-CDs, FA, and FA/N-CDs mixed system were examined. The peaks at 230 nm and 295 nm (black line) and two additional peaks at 280 nm and 350 nm (red line) in the absorption spectra could be assigned to N-CDs and FA (Fig. 5c) [40]. The perturbation at 350 nm of FA/N-CDs occurred, indicating that the ground-state complex between N-CDs and FA was formed through the hydrogen bond forces [41]. Therefore, the appearance of ground state complex and the slight shift of average lifetime between N-CDs and mixed system revealed the reliability of static quenching mechanism [42].

Moreover,the excitation spectra of N-CDs and the absorption spectra of FA were also studied to figure out the quenching mechanism. As Fig. 5b described, an overlap appeared between the maximum excitation peak of N-CDs at 295 nm and the maximum absorption peak of FA at 280 nm, which suggested that IFE was another significant factor contributing to this quenching process. And this process was further investigated by the Parker equation [43].
(3)
F
co
F
ob
=
2.3 γ A
ex
1 - 10
- γ
A
ex
10
β A
em
2.3 α A
em
1 - 10
-
α A
em

Here, F ob and F co are the observed intensity and the corrected intensity with removing IFE. A ex is absorbance per centimeter at the excitation wavelengths. A em is absorbance per centimeter at the emission wavelengths. “α”, “β”, and “γ” are the thickness of the excitation beam, the distance between the edge of the excitation beam, and the edge of cuvette and the cuvette width, respectively. The IFE effect increases with the increasing of the value of F co /F ob . With the increase of the FA concentration from 0 to 50.0 μM, Fco/Fob values increased from 1.17 to 1.93, matching the Parker equation[44]. In summary, the quenching mechanism of this CD-based fluorescent probe for effectively detecting FA could be attributed to the synergistic effect between static quenching and IFE (Fig. 5d) [45].

To explore the utility of these N-CDs in cells, effect of N-CDs on the cell viability was explored. The cell toxicity of N-CDs toward 4 T1 cells was investigated by conventional MTT method. The viability of 4 T1 cells remained above 95.0% even if the 4 T1 cells were incubated for 24 h in DMEM with 500.0 μg mL-1N-CDs (Fig. S9). Undoubtedly, these N-CDs caused little toxicity to the 4 T1 cells and could be exploited as an excellent reagent for cell imaging.

The capabilities of cell imaging were studied by confocal microscope. After incubated for 24 h with N-CDs, the strong blue fluorescence from the 4 T1 cells could be seen (Fig. 6 ), indicating that these N-CDs could efficiently enter 4 T1 cells through endocytosis owing to the small size of N-CDs [46]. Besides, fluorescence emission was almost disappeared after cells were cultured with N-CDs and FA (Fig. S10), indicating that the obtained N-CDs could enter 4 T1 cells and combine with the intracellular FA. No fluorescence quenching was found when the cells were cultured without FA. A further study on various time periods (0–80 min) displayed that the fluorescence of N-CDs scarcely declined (Fig. S11), demonstrating that N-CDs were highly stable to photobleaching. Generally, the high spatial and temporal resolution of this N-CDs based system for the intracellular detection of FA was mainly due to their small size, excellent dispersibility of these N-CDs and the high sensitivity for FA through the synergistic mechanism of static quenching and IFE. Therefore, these N-CDs could be applied as ideal materials for long-term monitoring of intracellular FA activity and fluorescence imaging.

In general, the as-prepared N-CDs were obtained from salicylic acid and urea via a convenient hydrothermal approach. The structural composition and luminescent characteristics of them were investigated through HR-TEM, FT-IR, XRD, XPS, absorption, and fluorescence spectra. They demonstrated various advantages, such as small particle diameter, good water dispersibility, higher photostability, and excellent biocompatibility. The initial fluorescence emission of N-CDs could be rapidly quenched by FA owing to static quenching mechanism and IFE. Apart from the excellent selectivity and high sensitivity for FA determination under laboratory conditions, the N-CDs also exhibited outstanding FA sensing in urine and FBS samples. Meanwhile, the as-prepared N-CDs successfully achieved the FA determination in cancer cells and cell imaging. In a word, the present work afforded a novel and green CD-based sensing strategy for the detection of small biological molecules, which would set a firm foundation for further biological applications of CDs.

Baoqiang Liu: Analyzed the data, Resources, Writing - original draft, Writing - review & editing. ShanShan Wei: Conceptualization, Writing - review & editing, Supervision. Enqi Liu: Supervision. Hongyuan Zhang: Software, Supervision. Pengju Lu: Conceptualization, Writing - review & editing. Jiali Wang: Conceptualization, Supervision. Guoying Sun: Conceptualization, Funding acquisition, Project administration, Writing - review & editing, Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This study was supported by the Jilin Province Science and Technology Development Project (20170101094JC).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.saa.2021.120661.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1
